stem: A 34-year-old primary school teacher attends her GP reporting six weeks of persistent low mood after the end of a 9-year relationship. She describes early morning waking at 0400 (two hours before her alarm), profound diurnal mood variation with mornings worst, weight loss of 4 kg without dieting, marked psychomotor retardation noted by her partner, and complete loss of mood reactivity even when her two cats greet her or her best friend visits. She is no longer able to plan lessons and has been on personal leave for three weeks. She denies hallucinations, sustained elevated mood, or any prior hypomanic episodes lasting more than a day. She drinks two standard drinks weekly, uses no other substances, and takes no regular medications. She reports passive thoughts of being better off dead but no plan or intent. Her TSH, FBC and UEC last month were unremarkable. PHQ-9 today is 23.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, congruent affect, slowed thought tempo, no thought disorder, no psychotic features.
data_table.Investigations: K-10 score 32. TSH 1.4 mIU/L, Hb 132 g/L, Na 139 mmol/L (all within reference).

lead_in: Which DSM-5 classification and specifier most accurately captures her presentation?

options.text: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) with intermittent major depressive episodes
options.rationale: PDD requires depressed mood most days for at least 2 years; 6-week course excludes this. Would be correct with 2+ year duration (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood, severe
options.rationale: Adjustment disorder is excluded once full MDE criteria are met; her symptom cluster meets MDE. Would be correct without meeting full MDE criteria (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Major depressive disorder, single episode, moderate severity, no specifier
options.rationale: Meets MDE but specific cluster (loss of mood reactivity, distinct quality, marked psychomotor retardation, early morning waking, diurnal variation) indicates melancholic specifier. Would be correct without melancholic features (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Major depressive disorder, single episode, severe, with melancholic features
options.rationale: She meets MDE criteria plus at least three melancholic features: complete loss of mood reactivity (cardinal feature A1), marked psychomotor retardation, early morning waking with diurnal mood variation worse in mornings, and significant weight loss. PHQ-9 23 plus functional collapse supports severe. Melancholic specifier matters: it predicts better response to TCAs/ECT and influences first-line drug choice under RANZCP guidance.

options.text: Bipolar II disorder, current episode depressed with melancholic features
options.rationale: Bipolar II requires at least one prior hypomanic episode lasting 4+ days; explicitly absent. Would be correct with documented hypomanic episode (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Sustained depressed mood plus anhedonia with neurovegetative features for 6 weeks meeting at least 5 SIGECAPS criteria with functional impairment satisfies DSM-5 MDD; absence of psychosis, mania or markedly impaired self-care places severity as moderate. RANZCP anchors management to severity bands.
key_points: MDD = 5+ of 9 SIGECAPS for >=2 weeks with functional impact and depressed mood OR anhedonia
K-10: 10-19 likely well, 20-24 mild, 25-29 moderate, 30-50 severe distress
Melancholic specifier needs anhedonia plus 3+ qualifying features
Adjustment disorder is excluded once MDD criteria are met
explanation.pearls: Distress score (K-10) and functional impact are separate axes; use both when grading severity (beyondblue, RANZCP).

subtopic_detail: Major depressive disorder - DSM-5 diagnosis and severity threshold

stem: A 22-year-old apprentice electrician presents with an 8-week history of moderate major depression (PHQ-9 16). He has had no manic episodes, no psychotic symptoms, and denies suicidal ideation. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily, drinks 4 standard drinks weekly, and uses cannabis socially once a month. His chief functional complaints are early morning waking at 0430, fatigue at work and a recent 3 kg weight loss. He is sexually active with his girlfriend and explicitly raises concerns about sexual side effects after reading about them online. He has no past medication trials, no allergies, no QTc concerns on a recent ECG (QTc 405 ms), and bloods including TSH, FBC, EUC, LFTs and B12 are unremarkable. His older sister had moderate depression that remitted well on sertraline. He and his GP agree to commence an antidepressant today alongside a Mental Health Treatment Plan for CBT.

data_table.Examination: Flat affect, no psychomotor agitation, no psychotic features, MSE otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 16, K-10 28, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, Hb 145 g/L, ALT 22 U/L, B12 360 pmol/L (all reference).

lead_in: Considering the eTG Psychotropic discriminators (efficacy, tolerability profile, sleep effect, sexual side-effect burden, family-history match), which is the single most appropriate first-line antidepressant?

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg orally nocte titrated to 30 mg
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has lower sexual side-effect burden and helps sleep but causes 2-4 kg weight gain and daytime sedation, poorly suited to apprentice work at heights. Would be correct in elderly or insomniac patient with no occupational concerns (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily
options.rationale: First-line SSRI with strong family-history concordance (a key eTG discriminator when comparing SSRIs), broad safety in young adults, no QTc dose-cap (unlike citalopram), and no significant CYP2D6 enzyme effect that would matter for his cannabis or future medications. Sexual side effects are a class effect that requires monitoring but does not exclude first-line SSRI; the response is to counsel, review at 2 weeks, and switch class only if intolerable.

options.text: Venlafaxine XR 75 mg orally daily
options.rationale: SNRI not first-line for uncomplicated moderate MDD; more difficult discontinuation profile and dose-dependent BP rise. Reserved for SSRI failure or specific indications. Would be correct after SSRI failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Reasonable SSRI but TGA QT-prolongation dose cap of 40 mg (20 in over-65 or on CYP2C19 inhibitor) limits future titration. Would be correct without family arrhythmia or QT concerns (Source: TGA).

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Acceptable SSRI but long half-life (norfluoxetine 7-15 days) complicates later switch to MAOI; sertraline preferred when family history may bear on switching. Would be correct in adolescents (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: eTG Psychotropic positions SSRIs as first-line for moderate MDD in adults, with sertraline, escitalopram and citalopram all reasonable starting choices. Family-history response is a soft predictor of personal response and is a clinically reasonable tiebreaker. Combine medication with psychological therapy via a Mental Health Treatment Plan.
key_points: First-line MDD: SSRI; sertraline, escitalopram, citalopram all reasonable
Sertraline: low interactions, broad therapeutic index, safe in young men
Mirtazapine helpful when insomnia + weight loss BUT not first-line if BMI normal
TCAs reserved due to overdose toxicity
MBS Mental Health Treatment Plan funds up to 10 sessions psychology per calendar year
explanation.pearls: Warn of an early agitation/anxiety phase in the first 1-2 weeks and arrange review at 1-2 weeks then 4 weeks; suicide risk monitoring is required especially in under-25s (Black Dog Institute).

subtopic_detail: MDD - first-line SSRI choice in a young adult

stem: A 42-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her partner with 6 hours of agitation, sweating and shivering. She was started on sertraline 100 mg daily six weeks ago for moderate depression. Two days ago she was prescribed tramadol 50 mg QID by another GP for acute lower back pain, and yesterday she began linezolid for a complicated skin infection at a sexual health clinic. On arrival she is restless and diaphoretic. She has spontaneous clonus in both ankles which is more pronounced on the right, mild rigidity in the lower limbs, hyperreflexia, mydriasis 5 mm bilaterally and an oral temperature of 38.6 deg C. There are no rashes, no neck stiffness and no focal neurological deficits. She has had no haloperidol or other antipsychotics, no recent anaesthetic and no head injury. She has not used St John's wort, MDMA or any other serotonergic agent her partner can identify. Her observations and bloods are recorded below.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/96 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, T 38.6 deg C, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6).
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, mydriasis 5 mm, lower-limb hyperreflexia, spontaneous clonus right > left, mild rigidity legs > arms, bowel sounds hyperactive.
data_table.Investigations: Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.1, creatinine 78 micromol/L, CK 540 U/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, lactate 2.1 mmol/L. ECG: sinus tachycardia, QTc 430 ms.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial step?

options.text: Cease serotonergics, IV fluids, benzodiazepine sedation, active cooling, admit for monitoring
options.rationale: She meets Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria (serotonergic agent + spontaneous clonus + agitation + diaphoresis + hyperthermia); cornerstone is removal of all serotonergic drugs, IV fluids, benzodiazepines for autonomic features, and active cooling for temperature >38.5 deg C (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

options.text: IV dantrolene with empirical intubation and ICU transfer
options.rationale: Dantrolene is for malignant hyperthermia, not recommended in serotonin syndrome; intubation is for refractory hyperthermia or airway compromise, neither present. Would be correct in malignant hyperthermia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IM haloperidol 5 mg for agitation with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Antipsychotics with dopamine antagonism can worsen autonomic instability and risk NMS; benzodiazepines are preferred sedative in serotonin toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Withhold sertraline only and continue tramadol with oral paracetamol
options.rationale: ALL serotonergic agents must be ceased (SSRI, tramadol, linezolid); paracetamol does not lower temperature (hyperthermia is muscle hyperactivity, not set-point). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected encephalitis
options.rationale: No neck stiffness, headache, focal neurology; clonus plus serotonergic drug history meets Hunter criteria; LP would delay treatment. Would be correct only with meningism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria identify serotonin syndrome via serotonergic exposure plus spontaneous clonus, OR inducible clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, OR ocular clonus + agitation/diaphoresis, OR tremor + hyperreflexia, OR hyperthermia >38 deg C + ocular/inducible clonus + rigidity. First-line is cessation of all serotonergic drugs, supportive care, benzodiazepines and active cooling.
key_points: Lower-limb hyperreflexia and clonus > upper limb (vs NMS lead-pipe rigidity)
Linezolid is a reversible MAOI - high risk with any SSRI/SNRI
Tramadol is serotonergic AND lowers seizure threshold
Cyproheptadine is option for refractory cases (specialist-led), not first-line
Hyperthermia from muscle hyperactivity - antipyretics ineffective
explanation.pearls: Symmetric lower-limb clonus plus diaphoresis on multiple serotonergics is the textbook triad - look for a tremor that continues after you let go of the foot.

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome - recognition and initial management

stem: A 48-year-old woman with a 9-month episode of MDD attends her psychiatrist for review. She started sertraline at 50 mg and titrated to 200 mg daily over 12 weeks with full adherence confirmed by webster pack; she completed an adequate trial with only partial response (PHQ-9 22 to 16). She was then switched to venlafaxine XR titrated to 225 mg daily over a further 10 weeks, again with partial response (PHQ-9 14). She has engaged consistently with a clinical psychologist for CBT throughout both trials. There has been no hypomania, no psychotic features and no substance use. She has hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily (BP today 128/82) and recent fasting lipids and HbA1c are normal. She has not had ECT or rTMS. She and her psychiatrist agree she meets criteria for treatment-resistant depression and discuss next steps. She is keen to avoid hospital admission. Her TSH is 1.6 mIU/L and B12 is 410 pmol/L. There are no acute suicidal features today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/82 mmHg, HR 78, weight 72 kg, BMI 25.
data_table.Examination: MSE: depressed mood, congruent affect, no psychomotor change, no psychotic features, passive death wishes only.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 14 on venlafaxine XR 225 mg. TSH 1.6 mIU/L, HbA1c 5.4%, fasting lipids normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Cross-titrate venlafaxine to paroxetine 20 mg daily as third SSRI/SNRI monotherapy
options.rationale: Already failed adequate trials of SSRI and SNRI; third monotherapy switch is less likely to produce remission than augmentation per STAR*D-style sequencing. Would be correct only with poor tolerability of current regimen (Source: STAR*D).

options.text: Add tranylcypromine 10 mg twice daily to her current venlafaxine therapy
options.rationale: Combining MAOI with SNRI is contraindicated (serotonin syndrome, hypertensive crisis); SNRI must cease with washout before MAOI. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Augment venlafaxine with aripiprazole 2 to 5 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Augmenting an SSRI/SNRI with a second-generation antipsychotic (aripiprazole, quetiapine, olanzapine) is supported by RANZCP CPG and eTG for partial response after two adequate trials; aripiprazole has the best metabolic profile and is started at low dose (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Add St John's wort 900 mg daily as a herbal augmentation strategy
options.rationale: St John's wort is serotonergic; combining with SNRI risks serotonin syndrome; also induces CYP3A4 with multiple interactions. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease venlafaxine abruptly and start moclobemide 300 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Abrupt venlafaxine cessation causes severe discontinuation; immediate switch to MAOI without washout (at least 1 week) risks serotonin syndrome. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant depression in Australia is commonly defined as failure of two adequate antidepressant trials. After two failed trials, evidence supports augmentation with a second-generation antipsychotic (aripiprazole, quetiapine, olanzapine) or lithium, switching antidepressant class, or moving to brain stimulation (ECT, rTMS). Aripiprazole augmentation is favoured for its metabolic profile.
key_points: Adequate trial = therapeutic dose, adequate duration (typically 4-8 weeks), confirmed adherence
First augmentation options: aripiprazole 2-5 mg, quetiapine XR 150-300 mg, lithium (target 0.6-0.8 mmol/L)
MAOI requires washout: 2 weeks most SSRIs, 5 weeks fluoxetine, 1+ week venlafaxine
ECT most effective for severe/psychotic/treatment-resistant MDD (RANZCP)
rTMS PBS-funded for TRD in adults (2021)
explanation.pearls: Always reconfirm adherence and dose adequacy before labelling TRD - apparent non-response is often under-dosing or covert non-adherence.

stem: A 71-year-old retired engineer is admitted involuntarily with severe major depression with psychotic features. He has held a fixed nihilistic delusion that his bowels have rotted for the past 5 weeks, refuses food and fluids, has lost 9 kg, and has required IV rehydration. He has had two adequate trials of antidepressants (sertraline 200 mg for 8 weeks, then mirtazapine 45 mg for 6 weeks with olanzapine 10 mg added for 4 weeks) without meaningful response, and his consultant proposes electroconvulsive therapy. He has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, an old uncomplicated inferior STEMI six years ago treated with primary PCI (most recent stress echo unremarkable), and a remote history of TIA with normal carotid imaging. He has no intracranial mass, no recent stroke, and no aneurysm. He is currently considered to lack capacity to consent to ECT and his adult daughter is the substitute decision-maker. Routine pre-ECT bloods are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 76, T 36.7 deg C, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: MSE: profound psychomotor retardation, nihilistic delusion, no perceptual disturbance, MMSE 24/30.
data_table.Investigations: Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2, creatinine 88 micromol/L, Hb 121 g/L, INR 1.0. ECG: sinus rhythm, old inferior Q waves, no ischaemia. Recent TTE: LVEF 55%.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate statement regarding ECT in this patient?

options.text: ECT is absolutely contraindicated because of his prior inferior myocardial infarction
options.rationale: ECT has NO absolute contraindications per RANZCP; recent MI (under 3 months), raised ICP and unstable aneurysms are relative; remote stable MI is not absolute. Would be correct only with recent MI under 3 months (Source: RANZCP ECT).

options.text: ECT is first-line for severe MDD with psychotic features and food refusal
options.rationale: RANZCP designates ECT as first-line for major depression with psychotic features, severe suicidality, life-threatening physical deterioration (refusal to eat/drink), catatonia, and treatment-resistant depression; this patient meets multiple indications (RANZCP Position Statement 74/76; Mood Disorders CPG).

options.text: ECT cannot lawfully proceed because he currently lacks capacity to consent
options.rationale: Lack of capacity does not preclude ECT; substitute decision-makers and tribunal authorisation pathways exist under state Mental Health Act. Would never be correct as absolute bar (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: All antidepressants must be ceased before ECT to avoid serotonin syndrome
options.rationale: Antidepressants are typically continued during ECT with case-by-case decisions; routine cessation not standard. Would be correct only for specific agents lowering seizure threshold (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Cognitive screening is unnecessary because he has no documented dementia diagnosis
options.rationale: Pre-ECT cognitive assessment is standard and should be repeated; MMSE 24 establishes baseline. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: ECT is a first-line option for severe MDD with psychotic features, severe suicidality, food refusal/dehydration, catatonia, or after two failed adequate trials. There are no absolute contraindications; relative contraindications include recent MI, raised ICP, unstable aneurysm and severe arrhythmia. Capacity issues are managed via state Mental Health Acts.
key_points: RANZCP first-line: psychotic depression, suicidal crisis, malnutrition/dehydration, catatonia, TRD
Pre-ECT workup: ECG, baseline cognition (MMSE/MoCA), anaesthetic review, medication reconciliation
Continue most antidepressants; lithium often held/reduced; benzodiazepines reduced (raise seizure threshold)
Common adverse effects: short-term memory loss (anterograde > retrograde), headache, myalgia
Bilateral ECT more effective but more cognitive impact than right unilateral
explanation.pearls: Cotard's nihilistic delusions (the patient believes they are dead or rotting) classically respond rapidly to ECT (RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG).

stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 0200 brought by police after his housemate found him writing letters to family members and packing personal effects into labelled boxes. He recently separated from his fiancee and was dismissed from his job in mining 3 weeks ago. He has a 6-month history of worsening depression treated by his GP with escitalopram 20 mg daily. Tonight he tells you he obtained his late father's licensed firearm from his mother's garage on the weekend and stored it under his bed. He has researched suicide methods online for the past fortnight, has selected a remote bush location, and has chosen tomorrow as the day. He denies prior attempts, has no current intoxication (breath alcohol 0.00), and identifies his younger sister and his dog as the only things keeping him alive at present. He has no psychotic features, no medical comorbidities, and no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 84, T 36.5 deg C, breath alcohol 0.00%, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: MSE: anxious, restricted affect, future plans focused only on death, no psychosis, insight partial, judgment impaired by hopelessness.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods unremarkable. Risk assessment summarised in stem.

lead_in: What is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: Discharge home with a written safety plan and beyondblue helpline contact details
options.rationale: Multiple high-risk features (specific plan, lethal means access, chosen date/location, recent stressors, hopelessness, final acts) make discharge unsafe. Would never be correct in active high-risk suicidality (Source: RANZCP Suicide Prevention).

options.text: Increase his escitalopram to 30 mg daily and reassess in clinic in one week
options.rationale: Dose escalation does not address acute 24-h risk; max escitalopram is 20 mg (10 mg elderly) and antidepressants can transiently worsen suicidality in young adults. Would never be correct as primary intervention (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Remove the firearm via police and admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Means restriction (especially of firearms) is the single most evidence-based intervention to reduce suicide death; combined with admission under the state Mental Health Act when at imminent risk, this is the priority action (Black Dog Institute; RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; NSW/Vic Mental Health Acts).

options.text: Administer IM olanzapine 10 mg for chemical restraint and continued observation
options.rationale: He is not agitated, aggressive or psychotic; chemical restraint is for behavioural emergencies, not suicide prevention. Would never be correct as primary strategy (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Have him sign a no-suicide contract and discharge to housemate supervision overnight
options.rationale: No-suicide contracts lack evidence of efficacy and may give false reassurance; lay supervision does not equate to clinical safety with loaded firearm access. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Highest-evidence interventions to prevent suicide are restriction of access to lethal means (firearms, pesticides, large medication supplies, jumping points), assessment and management of underlying disorders, and structured follow-up. A safety plan is a useful adjunct but not a substitute for means restriction or admission when risk is imminent.
key_points: Risk-magnifiers: specific plan, means access, chosen date/location, final acts, hopelessness, prior attempt, male, recent stressor, substance use, psychotic depression
Means restriction is the single most evidence-based intervention
Safety plan (Stanley/Brown): warning signs, internal coping, social distractions, contacts, professional/crisis services, means-restriction
Australian crisis: Lifeline 13 11 14, 13YARN 13 92 76, Suicide Call Back 1300 659 467, Kids Helpline 1800 55 1800
MHA admission criteria differ by state but include mental illness + risk to self/others + lack of less restrictive option
explanation.pearls: Always ask specifically about firearms in rural and ex-defence/police patients - they are the most lethal Australian suicide method per capita.

subtopic_detail: Suicide risk assessment and safety planning

stem: A 31-year-old graphic designer presents to her GP seeking help for recurring low mood. She describes three previous depressive episodes since age 22, each lasting 3 to 4 months and treated symptomatically. On detailed questioning she also describes recurrent 5-day periods in which she sleeps only 3 hours per night without fatigue, becomes unusually talkative, takes on new freelance projects she later abandons, spends $1500 to $2000 on art supplies, and is uncharacteristically socially disinhibited at parties. Her partner confirms these episodes are clearly different from her baseline, are noticeable to friends, and recur every 6 to 9 months. She has never been hospitalised for mental health issues, has never been psychotic, and has never required time off work during these elevated periods, which she actually finds productive. She uses no substances. TFTs, FBC and EUC are normal. She has no medical comorbidities and is not pregnant.

data_table.Examination: Currently euthymic-to-mildly low mood, reactive affect, no psychomotor change, no psychotic features, MMSE 30/30.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.5 mIU/L, Hb 132 g/L, urine beta-hCG negative. Urine drug screen negative.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits her presentation?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder, currently depressed phase
options.rationale: BPI requires manic episode at least 7 days OR requiring hospitalisation, often with marked impairment or psychosis; her elevated episodes are 5 days, non-impairing. Would be correct only with full mania (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Bipolar II disorder, currently depressed phase
options.rationale: Bipolar II requires a hypomanic episode (>=4 days, elevated mood + B-criteria, observable to others, not severe/hospitalised) PLUS a major depressive episode, with no history of mania - all met here (RANZCP CPG; ASBD).

options.text: Cyclothymic disorder with depressive features
options.rationale: Cyclothymia requires 2 years of numerous hypomanic and depressive symptoms NOT meeting full criteria; she has full episodes. Would be correct only with subthreshold episodes (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Recurrent major depressive disorder, moderate severity
options.rationale: Clear observable recurrent decreased need for sleep, increased goal activity, talkativeness and disinhibition exceed hypomania threshold; requires bipolar-spectrum diagnosis. Would be correct without hypomanic episodes (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Borderline personality disorder with affective instability
options.rationale: BPD has pervasive emotional instability hours-to-days, not discrete 5-day mood states; identity disturbance and abandonment fears not described. Would be correct in classic BPD (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Bipolar II diagnosis requires identifying hypomanic episodes that are often missed because they are ego-syntonic; collateral history is the key. Diagnostic confusion with recurrent MDD delays correct treatment, which materially changes pharmacological choice (mood stabiliser plus antidepressant cover, not antidepressant alone).
key_points: Hypomania: >=4 days, observable change, no severe impairment/hospitalisation, no psychosis
Mania: >=7 days OR hospitalisation, severe impairment +/- psychosis
Bipolar II = hypomania + MDE; never a manic episode
Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar II risks mood switching - mood stabiliser cover preferred
Quetiapine, lithium, lamotrigine are first-line maintenance in bipolar II (RANZCP/ASBD)
explanation.pearls: Always ask the partner: hypomania is most reliably detected by an outside observer, not the patient.

subtopic_detail: Bipolar I vs Bipolar II - diagnostic distinction

stem: A 38-year-old man with known bipolar I disorder is brought to the emergency department by his wife and police after 6 days of escalating behaviour. He has slept 90 minutes per night, has not eaten meaningfully for 3 days, has spent over $40,000 on cryptocurrency, has been propositioning strangers in shopping centres, and tonight stripped naked in the front yard while preaching to neighbours. In the ED he is loud, intrusive and grandiose, claiming to have been appointed Pope by direct revelation. He stopped taking his lithium 750 mg nocte six months ago because he felt better. He uses no illicit substances and a urine drug screen is negative. His wife reports that during previous severe episodes a mood stabiliser plus an antipsychotic worked well. He has no medical comorbidities and no antipsychotic allergies. He has been placed on an emergency assessment order under the state Mental Health Act and a bed on the acute psychiatric ward is being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/88 mmHg, HR 108, T 36.9 deg C, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: MSE: elevated mood, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, religious in content, no auditory hallucinations confirmed.
data_table.Investigations: Na 142 mmol/L, K 4.4, creatinine 78 micromol/L, eGFR >90, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, lithium level <0.1 mmol/L. ECG: sinus tachycardia, QTc 410 ms. Urine drug screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?

options.text: Sertraline 100 mg daily plus oral diazepam 10 mg TDS for agitation
options.rationale: Antidepressants are contraindicated in acute mania (prolong/worsen episode); benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat underlying illness. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Restart lithium 750 mg nocte as monotherapy and review in 5 days
options.rationale: Lithium has slow antimanic onset (5-7 days); monotherapy is inadequate for severe acute psychotic mania. Would be correct only in mild mania (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Sodium valproate 1500 mg orally daily as initial monotherapy strategy
options.rationale: Valproate monotherapy is option for moderate mania but combination with antipsychotic preferred for severe psychotic mania; valproate is high-risk in reproductive-age women. Would be correct only as adjunct in non-reproductive setting (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg orally daily plus restart lithium 750 mg nocte
options.rationale: RANZCP and eTG recommend combination of a mood stabiliser (lithium or valproate) with an atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone, aripiprazole) for severe acute mania especially with psychotic features; provides faster symptom control than monotherapy (RANZCP CPG 2020; eTG; ASBD).

options.text: Lamotrigine 25 mg orally daily titrated weekly as maintenance prophylaxis
options.rationale: Lamotrigine has no efficacy in acute mania; for prevention of bipolar depression with slow titration to mitigate SJS risk. Would be correct for bipolar depression prevention (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe acute mania with psychosis is treated with a mood stabiliser (lithium or valproate) combined with a second-generation antipsychotic for rapid behavioural control. Antidepressants are withheld during a manic episode. Admission, usually involuntary, is often required given impaired insight and disinhibition.
key_points: First-line acute mania: lithium OR valproate PLUS atypical antipsychotic
Antipsychotic options: olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone, aripiprazole, asenapine
Stop antidepressants (risk of prolonging or destabilising)
Adjunct benzodiazepine acceptable for agitation/insomnia short-term
Maintenance: continue mood stabiliser; lithium has suicide-protective effect (NNT ~14)
explanation.pearls: Always check a lithium level and a TFT on presentation - subtherapeutic levels often explain relapse, and lithium-induced hypothyroidism can mimic depressive relapse.

stem: A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stabilised on olanzapine 10 mg nocte and valproate 1000 mg daily following an inpatient admission for acute mania three months ago. She wishes to come off valproate because she and her partner plan to conceive in the next year, and her psychiatrist proposes substituting lithium for long-term maintenance. She has no renal disease, no cardiac history, no thyroid disorder, and is on no other medications. She drinks one cup of coffee per day and no alcohol. The psychiatrist plans to commence lithium carbonate 250 mg nocte and titrate to a serum level of 0.6 to 0.8 mmol/L for maintenance. She has read patient information about lithium and is concerned about tremor, weight change and the impact of dehydration on her dose. She and her partner are also discussing how she will manage acute illness with vomiting in future, and she has asked her psychiatrist to write a sick-day plan once treatment is established. Pre-initiation tests are now ordered.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 70, weight 64 kg, BMI 22.
data_table.Examination: MSE: euthymic, reactive affect, no psychomotor change, no psychotic features.

lead_in: Which combination of baseline investigations is most appropriate before commencing lithium?

options.text: Echocardiogram, 24-hour urinary creatinine clearance and dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: These are not routine pre-lithium investigations; standard serum creatinine/eGFR adequate for renal screening. Would never be correct (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Pregnancy test, lithium level and thyroid function tests as initial trio
options.rationale: Baseline lithium is irrelevant before initiation; renal function plus calcium plus ECG must also be obtained. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: FBC, UEC/eGFR, calcium, TFTs, pregnancy test and resting ECG
options.rationale: AMH and eTG Psychotropic require renal function (UEC, eGFR), thyroid function, calcium (lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism), pregnancy test (Category D - Ebstein anomaly risk), and ECG if cardiac risk factors or age > 40; FBC is reasonable baseline (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

options.text: Liver function tests, serum ammonia and gamma-glutamyl transferase as baseline
options.rationale: Lithium is renally cleared, not hepatically metabolised; LFTs and ammonia not required. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: FBC and UEC alone, with thyroid and calcium reviewed only after one year
options.rationale: Baseline TFTs and calcium are required at initiation to establish reference; monitored at 6-12 monthly intervals. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Pre-lithium baseline includes UEC/eGFR, TFTs, calcium, FBC, pregnancy test, and an ECG (mandatory > 40 years or with cardiac risk factors). Maintenance monitoring includes 12-hour post-dose lithium level at 5-7 days after initiation/dose change, then 3-6 monthly once stable, plus 6-12 monthly renal/thyroid/calcium.
key_points: Therapeutic level: 0.6-0.8 mmol/L maintenance; 0.8-1.0 mmol/L for refractory mania
Toxic > 1.5 mmol/L; severe > 2.0 mmol/L
Standard sample: trough level 12 hours after last dose
Lithium Category D (Ebstein anomaly risk in T1) - foetal cardiac echo at 20 weeks if continued
ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs, thiazides, dehydration all raise lithium levels
explanation.pearls: Counsel patients to maintain consistent salt and fluid intake and seek review with any vomiting, diarrhoea, fever, or new medication; subtle tremor, coarse tremor or ataxia signal toxicity.

stem: A 64-year-old woman maintained on lithium carbonate 500 mg nocte for bipolar I disorder for 12 years is brought to the emergency department by her daughter after 4 days of worsening coarse hand tremor, unsteady gait with two falls, slurred speech and confusion. Her usual psychiatrist documented her last lithium level 4 months ago as 0.7 mmol/L with normal renal function. Two weeks ago her GP commenced indapamide 2.5 mg daily for newly diagnosed hypertension and naproxen 500 mg BD for an osteoarthritic knee flare. She has been drinking less water than usual during a current heatwave (37 deg C ambient). She has no fever, no diarrhoea, no infective symptoms, and no chest pain. On arrival she is drowsy but rousable and has a coarse postural tremor, ataxic gait and mild dysarthria. There is no clonus, no rigidity, no diaphoresis and no mydriasis. ECG shows sinus rhythm with T-wave flattening. Initial bedside lithium level returns at 3.8 mmol/L, creatinine has risen from baseline 70 to 168 micromol/L and sodium is 142 mmol/L with no anion-gap acidosis.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 62, T 36.7 deg C, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, GCS 14 (E3 V5 M6).
data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor, mild dysarthria, broad-based ataxic gait, hyporeflexia bilaterally, no clonus, no rigidity.
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 2.4 mmol/L. Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.6, urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 78), eGFR 38. TSH 2.1 mIU/L. Calcium 2.42 mmol/L. VBG: pH 7.39, lactate 1.8. ECG: sinus rhythm, T-wave flattening, QTc 420 ms.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally and reduce lithium to half dose tonight
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium and is not used; lithium must be withheld not dose-reduced. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg and observation on the ward overnight
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation has minimal effect on lithium elimination; volume replacement is supportive but dialysis is for severe toxicity. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue lithium and recheck a 12-hour post-dose level in 6 hours
options.rationale: Continuing lithium in severe toxicity is dangerous; priority is removal and renal protection. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Withhold lithium and naproxen only, continue indapamide and IV fluids
options.rationale: All three (indapamide, NSAID, dehydration) must be addressed; thiazides increase proximal tubular lithium reabsorption raising levels. Would never be correct as partial (Source: AMH).

options.text: Withhold all three drugs, IV 0.9% saline, urgent dialysis with nephrology
options.rationale: Severe lithium toxicity (level > 2.5 mmol/L OR > 2.0 mmol/L with neurological symptoms OR > 1.5 mmol/L with renal impairment) is an indication for haemodialysis per Therapeutic Guidelines; she has neurological compromise, AKI and a 2.4 mmol/L level (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH; Austin Health Poisons).

explanation.summary: Chronic lithium toxicity is precipitated by reduced clearance (NSAIDs, thiazides, ACE inhibitors, dehydration, AKI). Severity is judged by clinical features and level. Haemodialysis indications: level > 4.0 mmol/L regardless of symptoms; > 2.5 mmol/L with severe neurological features or renal impairment; > 1.5 mmol/L with severe features and inability to excrete.
key_points: Mild toxicity (1.5-2.5 mmol/L): tremor, GI upset, weakness - withhold drug, IV saline, monitor
Severe (> 2.5 mmol/L or symptomatic with renal failure): dialysis
Common precipitants: NSAIDs, thiazides, ACEi/ARB, dehydration, low-salt diet, AKI
Lithium also causes nephrogenic DI, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism with chronic use
Levels rebound post-dialysis: serial levels and often a second session required
explanation.pearls: In a heatwave, counsel lithium patients to maintain hydration and consider holding the dose if vomiting or diarrhoea; brief breaks are safer than waiting until they are ataxic.

subtopic_detail: Lithium toxicity precipitated by drug interaction

stem: A 31-year-old primipara is brought to the emergency department by her partner on day 9 postpartum following a normal vaginal birth at 39+5 weeks of a healthy girl. Over the past 36 hours her partner reports rapid escalation of symptoms: she has slept only 2 hours in 48 hours despite the baby sleeping, is convinced the midwives implanted a tracker in the baby's umbilical stump, hears her late mother's voice instructing her, and at times is bright and over-talkative, at other times tearful and withdrawn. This morning she said the baby was sent by spirits and should be returned to them. She has not been violent but is intermittently dishevelled. She has no past psychiatric history but her older sister has bipolar I. There are no infective symptoms, no headache, no neurological signs and recent obstetric review and bloods are unremarkable. Her partner is willing to stay with her but has expressed serious safety concerns about leaving her unsupervised with the baby.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 92, T 36.8 deg C, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: MSE: labile affect, pressured speech alternating with whispering, paranoid and bizarre delusions, second-person auditory hallucinations, partial insight.
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, CRP 4 mg/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, calcium normal, urine MCS no growth, urine drug screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Reassurance, daily community midwife visits and sertraline 50 mg daily with GP review weekly
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis NOT PND; SSRI monotherapy and outpatient do not address imminent risk to mother/baby. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV aciclovir for suspected postpartum HSV encephalitis
options.rationale: Afebrile, no neuro deficit, normal CRP and strong bipolar family history make PP most likely; LP not first step. Would be correct only with infective features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral diazepam 10 mg and discharge home overnight with the partner supervising
options.rationale: Discharge of psychotic mother with infant at home is unsafe; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychosis. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

options.text: Admit to a Mother-Baby Unit, start antipsychotic, plan infant safety with partner
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with high suicide and infanticide risk; immediate admission (MBU preferred, general psychiatric ward if MBU unavailable) plus antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone), mood stabiliser if bipolar, and safety planning are standard (COPE; RANZCP; Black Dog Institute).

options.text: Repeat EPDS in 2 weeks and refer to perinatal psychologist for non-urgent assessment
options.rationale: EPDS screens for depression not psychosis; non-urgent referral is unsafe given imminent risk. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects 1-2 per 1000 births, typically presents in the first 2 weeks postpartum with rapid onset, fluctuating mood, confusion, paranoia and bizarre delusions often involving the baby. It is a psychiatric emergency with significant suicide and infanticide risk. Family history of bipolar disorder is a strong risk factor. Distinguish from baby blues (peaks day 3-5, self-limiting) and PND (gradual onset, mood-predominant).
key_points: Postpartum psychosis: onset typically days 3-14, abrupt, fluctuating, mixed mood/psychotic features
Bipolar family history is the strongest risk factor
Treatment: admit (MBU preferred), antipsychotic +/- mood stabiliser, ECT if severe/refractory
Postnatal depression: gradual onset, mood-predominant, screen with EPDS
Baby blues: peaks day 3-5, self-limiting <2 weeks, no specific treatment
explanation.pearls: Any new-onset psychosis in the first month postpartum is postpartum psychosis until proven otherwise - never discharge home overnight pending review.

stem: A 26-year-old accountant attends her GP for 9 months of persistent excessive worry across work, finances, parents' health and partner's safety. She describes feeling wound up most days, broken sleep with onset insomnia from rumination, muscle tension, irritability noted by her partner, and difficulty concentrating at work where her billable output has fallen by 25 percent. She has no panic attacks, no agoraphobia, no social anxiety pattern, no obsessions or compulsions, no trauma history and no anhedonia. K-10 is 24 and GAD-7 is 15 (moderate-to-severe). Bloods (TSH, FBC, EUC) are normal and caffeine intake is moderate (two coffees per day). She has no past psychiatric history, no past medication trials and no allergies. She is keen to engage with treatment but cannot start psychology for 4 weeks due to a clinician wait-list. She has private health insurance.

data_table.Examination: MSE: mildly anxious, no psychomotor change, no psychotic features, insight good.
data_table.Investigations: GAD-7 15 (moderate), K-10 24, TSH 1.7 mIU/L, Hb 134 g/L, Na 140 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line management plan?

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg twice daily for six weeks while she organises psychological therapy
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines not first-line in GAD (dependence risk, chronic course); short courses only for acute crisis. Would be correct only as short-term acute crisis bridge (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Trauma-focused CBT alone, deferring any pharmacotherapy until at least 12 weeks of therapy is complete
options.rationale: Trauma-focused CBT is for PTSD not GAD; she has no trauma. Generic CBT for GAD first-line for mild-moderate but GAD-7 15 with significant impairment warrants combined therapy. Would be correct in PTSD (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Commence escitalopram 10 mg daily plus arrange CBT, with review at 2 weeks for activation/akathisia and 6 weeks for response
options.rationale: GAD-7 15 plus 25 percent loss of work output places her in the moderate-severe band where eTG supports combined first-line therapy; escitalopram is a first-line SSRI for GAD with a favourable side-effect profile (under the 20 mg/day TGA QTc dose-cap), early review captures the 1-2 week activation window during which transient worsening may occur, and CBT remains the durable component.

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg orally nocte as initial pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Mirtazapine not first-line for GAD; SSRI/SNRI are first-line. Mirtazapine reserved for SSRI failure or specific sleep/appetite profile. Would be correct after SSRI failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Avoid caffeine completely and review in six months without other intervention today
options.rationale: Lifestyle alone insufficient for GAD-7 15 with functional impairment; mandates structured psychotherapy with/without SSRI. Would be correct only in mild GAD (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: GAD = excessive, hard-to-control worry on most days for at least 6 months PLUS at least 3 of restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance PLUS functional impairment. First-line is CBT; first-line pharmacotherapy when indicated is SSRI or SNRI, started low to mitigate initial anxiogenic effect.
key_points: CBT first-line for mild-moderate GAD
First-line drug: SSRI (escitalopram, sertraline) or SNRI (venlafaxine, duloxetine)
Start at half the usual depression-starting dose to mitigate initial agitation
Benzodiazepines: <2 weeks only and not first-line
Online CBT (e.g. This Way Up, MindSpot) is PBS-aligned and effective
explanation.pearls: GAD-7 cut-offs: 5 mild, 10 moderate, 15 severe; pair with functional impact when choosing intensity of intervention.

stem: A 28-year-old marketing manager has been to the emergency department three times in the past 8 weeks with episodes of sudden palpitations, chest tightness, shortness of breath, dizziness, paraesthesia in her hands and a feeling of impending doom. Each episode peaks within 10 minutes and resolves within 30. She is increasingly avoiding shopping centres and her work commute on the train, and now drives only short familiar routes. Her ECGs in the ED have been normal, troponins negative, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, electrolytes normal, plasma metanephrines (sent at her GP's request after the second presentation) normal, 24-hour Holter monitor normal, and a recent echocardiogram unremarkable. She drinks one coffee daily, no alcohol, no illicit drugs, no recent medication changes, and has no past medical history. She is normotensive at every visit (highest 128/82), euthyroid clinically, and has no tremor, no flushing, no headaches between episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 78, T 36.6 deg C, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: MSE: anxious about future episodes, no psychotic features. Cardiorespiratory exam normal.
data_table.Investigations: Multiple normal ECGs, troponins, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, EUC, plasma free metanephrines normal, Holter normal, TTE normal.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits her presentation?

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder, moderate severity
options.rationale: GAD has pervasive worry on most days for 6+ months across multiple domains, not discrete episodic surges with phobic avoidance. Would be correct in pervasive worry pattern (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Phaeochromocytoma with paroxysmal catecholamine surges
options.rationale: Plasma free metanephrines are screening test of choice and are normal; sustained/paroxysmal HTN, headaches, flushing also absent. Would be correct with elevated metanephrines and HTN (Source: APEG).

options.text: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with associated anxiety
options.rationale: Holter and ECGs in symptomatic visits have been normal; in absence of ECG documentation, panic disorder is most likely. Would be correct with ECG-documented paroxysmal AF (Source: Cardiology).

options.text: Panic disorder with associated agoraphobic avoidance behaviour
options.rationale: DSM-5 panic disorder = recurrent unexpected panic attacks (peak within minutes, 4+ symptoms) PLUS at least 1 month of worry about further attacks OR maladaptive behaviour change; agoraphobia is marked fear/avoidance of 2+ of 5 situations (public transport, open spaces, enclosed spaces, queues/crowds, alone outside home) (DSM-5; eTG; RANZCP).

options.text: Hyperthyroidism with adrenergic symptoms predominating
options.rationale: TSH is normal (1.8); no tremor, heat intolerance or weight loss to support thyrotoxicosis. Would be correct with suppressed TSH (Source: APEG).

explanation.summary: Panic disorder is diagnosed clinically once medical mimics (arrhythmia, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma, substance intoxication/withdrawal) have been reasonably excluded. Agoraphobia commonly co-exists and shapes treatment (exposure-based CBT critical). First-line treatment is CBT; first-line pharmacotherapy is an SSRI started at low dose.
key_points: Panic attack = abrupt surge of intense fear peaking within minutes, 4+ of 13 symptoms
Panic disorder = recurrent unexpected attacks + 1 month of worry or behaviour change
Agoraphobia: 2+ of public transport, open/enclosed spaces, queues/crowds, alone outside home
First-line: CBT with interoceptive and in vivo exposure; SSRI (sertraline, escitalopram) if drug indicated
Avoid PRN benzodiazepines - they undermine exposure learning
explanation.pearls: Patients with panic disorder frequently present to ED with chest pain; document the screening already done so future ED clinicians can move efficiently to reassurance and follow-up.

subtopic_detail: Panic disorder - diagnosis and discrimination from medical causes

stem: A 35-year-old paramedic presents to a psychologist for assessment. She describes spending 2 to 3 hours each evening repeatedly checking that the front door is locked, the gas is off, and that her ambulance bag is fully stocked. She acknowledges that the checking is excessive and irrational, finds the urge intensely distressing, and the rituals interfere with her sleep and family life. She also describes recurrent intrusive images of a fatal paediatric resuscitation she attended 18 months ago, occasional flashbacks triggered by sirens, and avoidance of certain suburbs where the call occurred. She no longer enjoys her work and has been on light duties for 4 months. She is not perfectionistic at baseline, does not collect lists or rules, is socially warm and flexible. She has no past psychiatric history, drinks 4 standard drinks per week, no illicit substances. Sleep is broken by both nightmares and rituals; mood is low but secondary to the above.

data_table.Examination: MSE: anxious, congruent affect, intrusive thoughts and avoidance, insight preserved, no psychotic features.
data_table.Investigations: K-10 27, PCL-5 49 (above clinical threshold). Bloods unremarkable.

lead_in: Which combination of diagnoses best fits her presentation?

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder as a single diagnosis explaining all features
options.rationale: OCPD is pervasive lifelong pattern of perfectionism/rigidity with limited insight (ego-syntonic); she lacks baseline perfectionism profile. Would be correct in lifelong perfectionism pattern (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder with intrusive rumination as the unifying construct
options.rationale: GAD has broad worry without ritualised compulsions or trauma re-experiencing; does not fully account. Would be correct in classic GAD (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder AND post-traumatic stress disorder, both meeting criteria
options.rationale: She meets DSM-5 OCD (intrusive, ego-dystonic obsessions and time-consuming compulsions with insight) AND PTSD (intrusive re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, hyperarousal lasting > 1 month after an index trauma); commonly comorbid in emergency-services workers (Phoenix Australia; RANZCP).

options.text: Post-traumatic stress disorder alone, with compulsive checking as a coping behaviour
options.rationale: PTSD captures re-experiencing/avoidance/hyperarousal but not ritualised compulsions which independently fit OCD; both present. Would never be correct alone (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Adjustment disorder with anxious mood and work-related stress as the trigger
options.rationale: Adjustment disorder applies when stressor causes symptoms NOT meeting another disorder; she meets criteria for two specific DSM-5 disorders. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: OCD features ego-dystonic obsessions and time-consuming compulsions with preserved insight, while OCPD is an ego-syntonic personality style of rigidity and perfectionism. PTSD requires an identifiable index trauma plus re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, and hyperarousal for > 1 month. Comorbidity is high in first responders.
key_points: OCD: ego-dystonic, time-consuming (>1 hr/day) compulsions, insight usually preserved
OCPD: ego-syntonic personality style, perfectionism, rigidity, present since young adulthood
PTSD: index trauma + 4 symptom clusters (re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, hyperarousal) > 1 month
Acute stress disorder: same features as PTSD but 3 days - 1 month
Phoenix Australia (formerly ACPMH) is the Australian PTSD authority
explanation.pearls: Always screen first responders, defence and police for both PTSD AND OCD plus AUD - comorbidity is the rule, not the exception.

subtopic_detail: OCD vs OCPD vs PTSD discrimination

stem: A 41-year-old former soldier presents to his GP 14 months after returning from a deployment where he witnessed a fatal IED incident. He describes nightly nightmares, intrusive images on most days, marked avoidance of news and crowds, persistent negative beliefs that the world is dangerous, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, insomnia and irritability. He has a PCL-5 score of 54 (above clinical threshold). He is not currently suicidal, is engaged in work part-time, drinks 6 standard drinks per evening (AUDIT 16) and uses no illicit substances. He is engaged with Open Arms Veterans and Families Counselling and is keen to consider trauma-focused therapy. He has not previously had pharmacotherapy or psychological treatment for PTSD. He has hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily (BP 132/82). He has no allergies, no cardiac disease and no significant past psychiatric history He has read about SSRIs for PTSD but is keen to try psychological treatment first if available.

data_table.Examination: MSE: anxious, hypervigilant, mood low, no psychotic features, no current SI.
data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 54, AUDIT 16, K-10 31, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LFTs ALT 38 (normal).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg as required for nightmares and hypervigilance episodes
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines not recommended in PTSD (impair fear extinction critical to trauma-focused therapy, worsen outcomes). Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD).

options.text: Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR with concurrent alcohol use disorder management
options.rationale: Phoenix Australia and RANZCP rank trauma-focused CBT (prolonged exposure, cognitive processing therapy) and EMDR as equally strong first-line treatments for PTSD with stronger evidence than pharmacotherapy; co-occurring AUD needs concurrent management (Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020).

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily as first-line monotherapy with weekly GP review
options.rationale: SSRIs and venlafaxine are second-line behind trauma-focused psychotherapy; reserved for psychotherapy unavailable, ineffective or refused. Would be correct only after psychotherapy unavailable (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Quetiapine 25 mg nocte for sleep and lifestyle advice only at this visit
options.rationale: Antipsychotics not first-line for core PTSD; low-dose quetiapine for sleep is unsupported with metabolic/movement-disorder risks. Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Critical incident stress debriefing within the next two weeks as single session
options.rationale: Single-session debriefing not recommended and may worsen outcomes; structured trauma-focused therapy over multiple sessions required. Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia).

explanation.summary: Phoenix Australia ranks trauma-focused CBT (prolonged exposure, cognitive processing therapy) and EMDR as equally strong first-line treatments for PTSD. Pharmacotherapy (SSRIs sertraline or paroxetine, or SNRI venlafaxine) is second-line or adjunctive. Co-occurring substance use disorder must be managed concurrently, not as a barrier to trauma therapy.
key_points: First-line PTSD: trauma-focused CBT OR EMDR
Second-line pharmacotherapy: sertraline, paroxetine, venlafaxine
Benzodiazepines: avoid (impair extinction learning)
Antipsychotics: not first-line; consider only for refractory psychotic symptoms
Open Arms Veterans and Families Counselling is the major Australian veteran service
explanation.pearls: Prazosin 1-15 mg nocte may help trauma nightmares in selected cases when therapy alone is insufficient (Phoenix Australia).

stem: A 24-year-old violinist auditioning for an orchestral position presents to her GP 4 weeks before her audition. She describes intense fear of trembling hands, sweating and a quavering voice during public performance, but is socially comfortable in everyday interactions, meeting new people, and giving casual presentations at her teaching work. She has never had a panic attack outside performance situations, and has no avoidance of social settings, no generalised worry, no obsessive-compulsive features and no trauma history. She has previously taken a beta blocker borrowed from a colleague before one audition with good effect but is unsure of the dose. She has no asthma, no diabetes, no heart block, no hypotension and no medication allergies. She has access to a psychologist through a Mental Health Treatment Plan and is open to longer-term therapy after the audition. Resting HR 78, BP 116/74, ECG sinus rhythm with normal PR interval.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA, BMI 22.
data_table.Examination: MSE: mildly anxious about audition, otherwise normal. Cardiorespiratory exam normal.
data_table.Investigations: Resting ECG sinus rhythm rate 78, PR 152 ms. TSH 1.6 mIU/L, EUC normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management for the audition?

options.text: Propranolol 10-40 mg orally pre-event PRN, with CBT referral for longer-term care
options.rationale: Performance-only social anxiety responds well to short-term beta blockade (propranolol 10-40 mg about 1 hour pre-event) which blunts peripheral autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) without affecting cognition; CBT is first-line for longer-term/generalised social anxiety (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally one hour before the performance as a single dose
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines impair fine motor performance and cognition; not preferred where sensorimotor task required. Would be correct only in non-motor performance with high distress (Source: AMH).

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily commencing immediately for ongoing daily use
options.rationale: SSRIs are first-line for generalised social anxiety but take 4-6 weeks and can paradoxically worsen anxiety in first 1-2 weeks; not appropriate for single performance. Would be correct in generalised SAD (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Quetiapine 25 mg orally as required prior to performance for anxiolysis
options.rationale: Quetiapine not indicated for performance anxiety; sedation impairs fine motor control and metabolic effects outweigh benefit. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Cannabidiol oil orally prior to the performance as an alternative anxiolytic
options.rationale: CBD does not have established evidence for performance anxiety in AU; quality control and PBS access limiting. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: DSM-5 social anxiety disorder has a performance-only specifier when fear is restricted to public performance. Episodic, situation-specific symptoms with no general social impairment respond well to beta blockade (propranolol) used as required prior to events. Generalised social anxiety is treated with CBT first-line; SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline, escitalopram) are first-line pharmacotherapy.
key_points: Performance subtype: propranolol 10-40 mg orally 30-60 min pre-event PRN
Generalised social anxiety: CBT first-line; SSRI/SNRI if drug indicated
Avoid beta blockers in asthma, heart block, hypotension, decompensated heart failure
Avoid benzodiazepines for performance (motor/cognitive impairment)
Always pair short-term symptomatic relief with referral for ongoing therapy
explanation.pearls: Trial the propranolol at a similar low-stakes event before the actual audition - some patients get marked bradycardia or fatigue at higher doses.

subtopic_detail: Social anxiety disorder - performance subtype management

stem: A 36-year-old man presents to his GP 4 days after stopping venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily, which he had taken for 8 months for moderate depression. He stopped abruptly while on a 5-day camping trip after forgetting to pack his medication. Over the past 48 hours he has developed flu-like symptoms with no fever, dizziness on standing, brief electric-shock sensations in his head when he turns it (brain zaps), nausea, insomnia with vivid dreams, low mood, irritability and anxiety. He has not had any new infectious contacts, no head trauma, no headache and no neurological deficit. He has no chest pain, no palpitations, no diarrhoea or vomiting. He still has his original venlafaxine prescription at home. His depression had remitted well on treatment and he had not yet discussed planned cessation with his GP. He has no other medications, no allergies and no significant past medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 120/78 mmHg lying, 108/70 standing, HR 80 lying, 96 standing, T 36.6 deg C, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Mildly tremulous, normal neurological exam, no neck stiffness, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, CRP unremarkable. ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure as depressive relapse and start mirtazapine 30 mg nocte tonight
options.rationale: Onset within days of cessation, somatic and sensory features (brain zaps, dizziness) fit discontinuation syndrome not relapse (which takes weeks). Would be correct only if symptoms weeks after cessation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected meningitis
options.rationale: Afebrile, no neck stiffness, no headache, no rash, clear pharmacological explanation. Would be correct with meningism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Admit for IV fluids and treat empirically as viral gastroenteritis overnight
options.rationale: Mild nausea without vomiting/diarrhoea and normal bloods do not warrant admission, and misses diagnosis. Would be correct only with significant GI illness (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Restart venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily and plan a gradual taper with GP
options.rationale: These FINISH features (Flu-like, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbance/brain zaps, Hyperarousal) within days of stopping a short-half-life serotonergic agent are classic antidepressant discontinuation syndrome; restarting the original drug typically resolves symptoms within 24-72 hours, then slow taper over weeks (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

options.text: Increase venlafaxine to 225 mg daily once restarted and review in two weeks
options.rationale: No evidence of inadequate treatment response; reinstating previous effective dose is appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Discontinuation syndrome occurs within days of stopping or markedly reducing a serotonergic antidepressant, especially short-half-life agents (paroxetine, venlafaxine). The FINISH mnemonic captures the clinical picture: Flu-like, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbance (brain zaps), Hyperarousal/anxiety. Distinguish from relapse by onset within days, sensory features, and rapid resolution on reinstating the drug.
key_points: Highest risk: paroxetine, venlafaxine (short half-lives); lowest: fluoxetine
FINISH features begin within 1-7 days, peak within 1 week
Restart same drug at previous dose; resolves within 24-72 hours
Taper over weeks (longer for paroxetine, venlafaxine); slower in elderly and long-term users
Distinguish from relapse: relapse takes weeks-months and lacks sensory/flu-like features
explanation.pearls: Counsel every antidepressant patient at initiation that abrupt cessation can cause withdrawal-like symptoms; a planned taper at the time of discontinuation reduces unplanned cessation.

stem: A 74-year-old retired accountant is brought to clinic by his daughter with a 4-month history of progressively worsening low mood, total loss of interest in his lifelong hobby of bridge, marked early morning waking by 0330, 8 kg unintentional weight loss, profound guilt that he is ruining the family financially despite stable superannuation, and pervasive slowing of speech and movement noted by family. He describes his mood as distinctly worse in the morning, lifting somewhat by afternoon. He has had passive suicidal thoughts but no plan. He has not had hallucinations or delusions. He has had two previous depressive episodes in his 50s, both responding to escitalopram. His current medications are ramipril 5 mg, atorvastatin 20 mg, and metformin 1 g BD. Bloods including TSH, FBC, EUC, LFTs, B12, folate, calcium and HbA1c are unremarkable. MMSE today is 27/30 (lost 2 on serial sevens, 1 on recall, attributed to concentration not cognitive decline). His daughter is highly engaged and able to provide reliable collateral history covering the last 6 months His daughter notes he had no reactivity to a recent visit from his grandchildren and ate nothing at the family dinner.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 64, weight 68 kg (down from 76).
data_table.Examination: MSE: severe psychomotor retardation, monotone speech, depressed affect, mood-congruent worthlessness; no psychosis. MMSE 27/30.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.6, B12 410, folate 18, calcium 2.38, HbA1c 6.8%, eGFR 78. ECG sinus 60 QTc 420 ms.

lead_in: Which feature most specifically supports an MDD specifier of melancholic features?

options.text: Past responsiveness of his prior depressive episodes to escitalopram therapy
options.rationale: Treatment response history informs choice but is not part of melancholic specifier criteria. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Hyperphagia and hypersomnia accompanied by leaden paralysis sensation
options.rationale: These describe atypical features (mood reactivity, increased appetite/weight, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis), the opposite specifier. Would be correct for atypical specifier (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Patient demographic factors including age over 65 and recent retirement
options.rationale: Demographic factors not part of specifier criteria, although melancholic is more common in older adults. Would never be a criterion (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Anhedonia plus AM-worse diurnal mood, early waking, retardation and guilt
options.rationale: DSM-5 melancholic features require loss of pleasure in nearly all activities (or lack of reactivity to pleasurable stimuli) PLUS at least three of: distinct quality of depressed mood, morning-worse mood, early morning waking >=2 hours, marked psychomotor change, significant weight loss, excessive guilt - all present here (RANZCP CPG; DSM-5).

options.text: Presence of passive suicidal ideation without any specific plan or intent
options.rationale: Suicidal ideation contributes to severity but is not melancholic-specifier feature. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

explanation.summary: Melancholic depression is characterised by anhedonia + biological symptoms (early waking, morning-worse mood, weight loss, psychomotor change) + excessive guilt. It is more common in older adults, has a stronger biological substrate, less placebo response, and a relatively better response to TCAs and ECT compared to atypical features. Distinguish from pseudodementia (improves with treatment, no progressive cognitive decline).
key_points: Melancholic specifier: anhedonia + >=3 of distinct mood, AM-worse, EM waking >=2h, marked psychomotor change, weight loss/anorexia, excessive guilt
Atypical specifier: mood reactivity + >=2 of weight gain/appetite increase, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity
Older-adult MDD: lower MMSE may reflect pseudodementia - reassess after treatment
ECT highly effective in melancholic and psychotic depression
Always rule out organic mimics (thyroid, B12, calcium, hypoactive delirium)
explanation.pearls: If MMSE rises by >=2 after successful treatment of depression, the original cognitive decline was likely pseudodementia, not an early dementia.

subtopic_detail: MDD with melancholic features in older adult

stem: A 28-year-old woman has had recurrent depression since adolescence. She describes that her mood lifts noticeably when good things happen (a friend visits, she gets praised at work) but plunges back within hours. She sleeps 11 hours daily including a 2-hour afternoon nap, has gained 12 kg in 9 months, experiences a profound leaden paralysis sensation in her limbs that makes physical activity feel impossible, and is acutely sensitive to perceived criticism or rejection (a tone of voice from her supervisor preoccupies her for days). She has had two prior trials of antidepressants: sertraline 100 mg for 10 weeks (PHQ-9 16 to 12) and escitalopram 20 mg for 12 weeks (no further improvement). She has no manic or hypomanic episodes, no psychotic features, no substance use, no eating disorder. She has well-controlled hypothyroidism on thyroxine 75 mcg with TSH 1.8.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 72, weight 84 kg, BMI 30.
data_table.Examination: MSE: depressed mood with episodic brightening, weight gain, hypersomnolence, leaden affect; no psychotic features. MMSE 30/30.
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 18, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, FBC, UEC, LFTs unremarkable, fasting BSL 5.2, HbA1c 5.6%.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Add nortriptyline 50 mg orally nocte to her current escitalopram therapy
options.rationale: Adding TCA to SSRI risks elevated TCA levels (CYP2D6 inhibition) and is not the recommended next step in atypical depression; evidence favours MAOI. Would be correct after MAOI contraindication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Increase escitalopram beyond licensed maximum and add bupropion adjunct
options.rationale: Exceeding licensed maximum dose risks QT prolongation without proven benefit; bupropion not registered for depression in AU (only Zyban for smoking cessation). Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

options.text: Cross-taper to phenelzine MAOI with washout, dietary restriction and specialist care
options.rationale: Atypical depression features (mood reactivity, hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity) classically respond preferentially to MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine) over SSRIs/TCAs; after two failed SSRI trials, escalation under specialist supervision with washout and dietary education is appropriate (eTG Psychotropic; AMH; RANZCP CPG).

options.text: Substitute mirtazapine 30 mg orally nocte for her current escitalopram dose
options.rationale: Mirtazapine causes further weight gain and sedation, worsening existing weight gain and hypersomnia; lacks specific evidence for atypical. Would be correct only without weight/sleep concerns (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add levothyroxine 25 mcg to her current dose despite her normal TSH
options.rationale: T4 augmentation in euthyroid patient has no evidence for atypical features and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis; T3 is the augmentation agent in TRD. Would be correct only with T3 (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Atypical depression (DSM-5 specifier) = mood reactivity + 2 or more of significant weight gain or appetite increase, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, long-standing interpersonal rejection sensitivity. Historical RCTs (Liebowitz) demonstrated superior response to MAOIs over TCAs in atypical depression. After failure of two adequate SSRI trials, escalation to an MAOI under specialist supervision is a defensible next step, with strict attention to washout periods and dietary restriction.
key_points: Atypical features: mood reactivity + >=2 of weight gain/hyperphagia, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity
MAOIs: phenelzine, tranylcypromine, moclobemide (RIMA - less dietary restriction)
MAOI washouts: 2 weeks from most SSRIs, 5 weeks from fluoxetine, 1+ week from SNRI
Tyramine-rich foods to avoid with irreversible MAOIs: aged cheese, cured meats, fermented soy, broad bean pods, draught beer
Always check for occult bipolarity (atypical depression look-alike in young women)
explanation.pearls: If atypical features are accompanied by hypomanic episodes, the diagnosis is bipolar II and an MAOI is inappropriate without mood stabiliser cover - re-screen carefully before initiation.

subtopic_detail: Atypical depression - feature recognition and MAOI escalation
